amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: E. coli maintains metal homeostasis through tightly regulated import, export, and sequestration systems. Copper homeostasis involves the CusSCFBA efflux system (regulated by CusRS two-component system) that exports excess Cu+ and Ag+, and the CopA P-type ATPase that pumps Cu+ into the periplasm. Zinc homeostasis is controlled by the ZnuABC high-affinity importer (regulated by Zur repressor under zinc limitation) and the ZntA P-type ATPase exporter (regulated by ZntR activator under zinc excess). Both systems involve metal-sensing transcriptional regulators with metal-binding sites that change DNA affinity upon metal coordination. This flowchart illustrates copper and zinc sensing, regulatory responses, and the transport systems that maintain non-toxic intracellular metal concentrations essential for metalloenzyme function while preventing toxicity.

    A4 --> A60[Low Zn2+ in cytoplasm]

    A74 --> A75{ZnuABC complex formation}

    A75 -->|Incomplete| Wait2[Wait for synthesis]
    Wait2 --> A75
    
    A76 --> A77[ZnuA binds Zn2+ in periplasm high affinity]
    A77 --> A78[ZnuA delivers Zn2+ to ZnuB]

    A79 --> A80[Zn2+ transported into cytoplasm]
    
    A80 --> A81{Zinc sufficient?}
    A81 -->|Yes - enough Zn| A82[Zn2+ binds to Zur]
    A81 -->|No - still limiting| A83[Maintain import]
    A82 --> A64
    
    A32 --> A84[Copper homeostasis restored]
    A59 --> A85[Zinc homeostasis restored]
    A80 --> A86[Zinc acquisition for metalloenzymes]
    
    A84 --> End1([Metal resistance - survival])
    A85 --> End2([Metal homeostasis maintained])
    A86 --> End3([Zinc-dependent enzymes functional])
    
    A37 --> End2
    A68 --> End3
    A35 --> A87[Chronic copper stress]

    A87 --> A90[Continued efflux or cell death]
    A88 --> A90
    A89 --> A91[Impaired metalloenzyme function]
    A91 --> A92[Growth defect]

name: Heavy Metal Resistance (Copper and Zinc Homeostasis)
notes: Metal homeostasis illustrates the dual challenge bacteria face: acquiring essential metals (Zn for metalloenzymes) while preventing toxicity from excess. The reciprocal regulation of ZnuABC (import, repressed by Zur when Zn high) and ZntA (export, activated by ZntR when Zn high) creates homeostasis. Copper is particularly toxic due to Fenton chemistry generating ROS. The Cus system is notable for handling both Cu+ and Ag+. These systems are important for pathogen survival in hosts (metal withholding by immune system) and for bioremediation applications. The metal-sensing mechanisms (conformational changes upon metal binding) are paradigms for allosteric regulation.
scientificAccuracy: This flowchart is based on extensive biochemical and genetic characterization of E. coli metal homeostasis systems. All transporters (CusSCFBA, CopA, ZnuABC, ZntA), regulators (CusRS, Zur, ZntR), and mechanisms have been validated through structural biology and functional studies. The model represents bacterial strategies for essential metal acquisition and toxic metal resistance.
